data.title: Israel says it is open for dialogue with Lebanon, but will continue to target Hezbollah
data.description:
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said Thursday that he has authorized direct talks with Lebanon focused on disarming Hezbollah and “the formalization of peaceful relations” between the two countries, marking the first such diplomatic effort since U.S.-mediated negotiations collapsed in 1983, the Washington Post reported.

The announcement came after Lebanese Prime Minister Nawaf Salam called for state military and security forces to “immediately reinforce the state’s full control” over Beirut, ensuring weapons remain only in the hands of “legitimate security forces.”

Despite the diplomatic opening, Israel has continued its bombing campaign against Hezbollah, striking over 100 targets in Beirut on Wednesday and killing more than 200 people, Lebanese authorities said.

“We continue to strike Hezbollah with force, precision, and determination,” Netanyahu posted on X. “We will continue to strike Hezbollah wherever required, until we restore full security to the residents of the north.”

The ongoing military offensive appears to contradict a two-week truce agreement with Iran brokered by the U.S. and Pakistan, which mediators say includes Lebanon.

Iranian leader Masoud Pezeshkian said the strikes were a “blatant violation of” and “dangerous sign of deception and lack of commitment to” the ceasefire agreement, warning that “the continuation of these actions will render negotiations meaningless.”

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Accenture’s AI spending spree signals underestimated upside, UBS says
data.description:
Accenture (ACN) is moving with unusual clarity in artificial intelligence, and UBS analyst Kevin McVeigh says the market isn’t fully appreciating its strategy.

In a research note following Accenture’s (ACN) latest acquisition activity, McVeigh framed the purchase of Spain-based Keepler Data Tech less as a one-off deal and more as evidence of disciplined execution around a broader AI strategy. The addition of more than 240 specialists across data science, machine learning and cloud architecture strengthens Accenture’s (ACN) ability to deliver across the full data and AI lifecycle, particularly in Europe.

For McVeigh, the significance lies in how these capabilities align with rising enterprise demand for scalable and responsible AI deployment, an area where large clients increasingly want end-to-end partners rather than fragmented vendors.

$5B M&A push seen as strategic

McVeigh pointed out that Accenture (ACN) has raised its fiscal 2026 acquisition target to $5 billion, up from $3 billion earlier in the year, and is already well on its way. The company has deployed about $1.6 billion across multiple deals, including NeuraFlash, Halfspace and Decho.

Rather than viewing this as aggressive spending, UBS sees a deliberate shift toward higher-growth and higher-margin business lines. The firm is prioritizing models that rely less on traditional labor-intensive consulting and more on scalable, technology-driven offerings.

That transition, McVeigh argues, could ultimately change how investors value the company, yet current market pricing does not reflect that potential.

AI revenue growth

A central pillar of the UBS thesis is Accenture’s (ACN) ability to monetize artificial intelligence at a pace that exceeds even its earlier cloud growth cycle.

Since launching its generative AI initiatives in fiscal 2023, the company has grown related revenue to about $2.7 billion by fiscal 2025, implying roughly 200% compound annual growth. That compares with about 132% growth during the early years of its cloud business.

Bookings tied to AI and data partnerships are also expected to more than double in fiscal 2026, reinforcing what UBS describes as strong demand momentum.

Margin expansion

McVeigh sees three factors working together to drive both revenue acceleration and higher margins: record bookings tied to AI, a $5 billion acquisition pipeline focused on high-conviction themes, and a workforce that now includes more than 85,000 AI professionals.

Taken together, UBS believes these elements position Accenture for a structural improvement in profitability, not just cyclical growth.

The key takeaway from McVeigh’s note is not the acquisition itself, but what it represents: a coordinated push into AI that could reshape the company’s growth profile. For now, he argues, investors have yet to fully price that shift.

data.title: Justice Department investigating NFL broadcast practices—Wall Street Journal
data.description:
The Department of Justice is investigating whether the National Football League’s policy of splitting its broadcast privileges is anticompetitive and harms consumers, The Wall Street Journal reported.

While the Sports Broadcasting Act of 1961 grants the NFL limited antitrust protection to negotiate TV rights, the practice makes it difficult for football fans to watch games that are broadcast on streaming services that require a subscription.

Prior to the proliferation of streaming services, NFL games were broadcast across the major network channels, giving fans free access to their favorite teams. The entire season is now split across several channels, including CBS (PSKY), ABC (DIS), NBC (CMCSA), and Fox (FOX) (FOXA), and streaming services like Disney’s ESPN (ESPN), Netflix (NFLX), Prime Video (AMZN), YouTube TV (GOOG), Peacock (CMCSA), and Paramount+ (PSKY) with each taking a piece of the entire NFL package.

Accordingly, the Federal Communications Commission sought public comment on how this arrangement impacted fans, prompting Senator Mike Lee (R-UT) as chairman of the Senate Judiciary Subcommittee on Antitrust, Competition Policy and Consumer Rights, to request a review of the NFL by the DOJ.

“To watch every NFL game during the past season, football fans will spend almost $1,000 on cable and streaming subscriptions,” Lee wrote in his letter to the DOJ.

The NFL defends its position by emphasizing that because 87% of games are broadcast on each team’s local TV channel on game day, the NFL is the most “fan-friendly” of the major sports leagues.

data.title: ClearBridge Dividend outperforms in Q1, adds new positions, and exits key holdings
data.description:
• The ClearBridge Dividend Strategy (SOPAX) outperformed the S&P 500 in Q1 2026, as the benchmark fell 4.3% while the portfolio was broadly flat on a gross-of-fees basis.
  • The fund established new positions in Blackstone (NYSE: BX), Otis Worldwide (NYSE: OTIS), Honeywell International (NASDAQ: HON), and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (NYSE: TSM).
  • The fund fully exited Northrop Grumman (NYSE: NOC) and Oracle (NYSE: ORCL).
  • Source.

data.title: Invivyd rises after update from late-stage trial for COVID therapy
data.description:
Invivyd (IVVD) shares rose on Thursday after the Waltham, Massachusetts-based biotech announced progress in a late-stage trial for VYD2311, its experimental therapy to prevent COVID-19 symptoms.

Based on an interim analysis of its Phase 3 pivotal study, DECLARATION, the company stated that the trial has already recorded sufficient clinical events, which could support the statistical power required to prove the high end of VYD231's targeted efficacy levels.

The sample-size re-estimation analysis, which was added to improve the study’s statistical power and conducted on April 6 (Day 45), has triggered upsizing of the trial by nearly 500 additional enrollments, Invivyd (IVVD) noted.

As a result, subject to recruitment rates, the company said that it shifted the timeline to share DECLARATION’s topline data by roughly two months, to Q3 2026 from mid-2026.

“Our conservative upsizing algorithm means that we can move to higher confidence in statistical power with very modest additional time,” Invivyd (IVVD) chairman Marc Elia added.

data.title: U.S. stocks in green territory while U.S.-Iran resolution is still uncertain
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages pared losses on Thursday as hopes of a resolution to the U.S.-Iran conflict waned following warnings from President Donald Trump of a major escalation.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +0.7%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +0.8%, and the Dow (DJI) was +0.8%.

Over in the bond market, the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 4 basis points lower at 4.26%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) dropped 4 basis points to 3.76%.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) rose again and were last at around $96.7, while Brent (CO1:COM) was also higher at $95.5 per barrel.

“All U.S. Ships, Aircraft, and Military Personnel…and anything else that is appropriate and necessary for the lethal prosecution and destruction of an already substantially degraded Enemy, will remain in place in, and around, Iran, until such time as the REAL AGREEMENT reached is fully complied with,” Trump said on Truth Social.

As the U.S. agreed to a ceasefire in the Middle East, conditional on Iran agreeing to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, Tehran also reportedly agreed to the two-week deal as long as all attacks were stopped, according to Iran’s foreign minister.

However, Iran’s parliamentary speaker, Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, has accused both the U.S. and Israel of violating three elements of the 10-point ceasefire deal, including continued attacks on Lebanon, a drone in Iranian airspace, and denying them "the right to enrich uranium."

In economic news, the February Core PCE Price Index came in at +0.4% MoM vs. +0.3% consensus and +0.4% prior. In addition, the PCE Price Index came in at +0.4% MoM vs. +0.4% consensus and +0.3% prior.

The initial jobless claims rose more than expected in the past week, while the U.S. Q4 GDP growth estimate was further revised down to +0.5%.

Also, mortgage rates inched down, averaging 6.37% for the 30-year fixed rate and 5.74% for the 15-year fixed rate, after five consecutive weeks of rising, according to the latest Freddie Mac Primary Mortgage Survey.

Wholesale inventories climbed 0.8% MoM in February, vs. the -0.5% consensus, the U.S. Census Bureau said.

“We are certain that American central bankers will not [dismiss the latest economic data], given the 3.7% three-month average annualized pace of core inflation and the ugly inflation-adjusted income and savings dynamics inside the February report,” said Joseph Brusuelas, RSM U.S. principal and chief economist.

“While the topline increase of 0.1% and 2.8% is pre-war data and treated as stale, given the oil shock that will send it soaring in the March data, the increase in the core metric—the best long-run predictor of overall inflation—is far more problematic for central bankers that are now six years into a journey well above the Fed’s 2 percent inflation target, whether the ceasefire in the Gulf holds or hostilities resume in the near term,” Brusuelas said.

data.title: Global economy faces rising inflation and slower growth – IMF’s director
data.description:
The global economy faces a dual threat of rising inflation and slower growth in the wake of the Iran war, according to International Monetary Fund Managing Director Kristalina Georgieva.

In an interview with CNBC, Georgieva warned that the conflict represents a “negative supply shock” that is pushing prices up worldwide, with the severity of the damage depending on whether the current ceasefire holds and the extent of infrastructure destruction already inflicted.

“All roads go into higher inflation, slower growth,” Georgieva said, explaining that the IMF has scrapped a previously expected 0.1% upgrade to global growth projections.

Instead, the fund will present three different scenarios next week based on how the crisis unfolds. If hostilities persist, she cautioned, “inflation starts flashing red signs, then central banks have to step in,” which would further suppress economic growth.

Georgieva emphasized that while the crisis is global in scope, its impact is far from uniform.

“This crisis is global, but it is very asymmetric,” she said, noting that oil exporters like the U.S. are in a “much more protected position,” while poor oil-importing countries find themselves “in a very tough spot.”

The most acute pain is being felt in Asia, particularly in nations with weak financial reserves. Countries like the Philippines and Thailand are already experiencing fuel rationing due to their limited reserves.

By contrast, major economies like Japan and China, while also energy importers, have substantial oil and gas reserves that provide a buffer against supply disruptions.

The IMF is preparing a substantial financial response, estimating that affected nations will need between $20B and $50B in additional financing. The fund already has programs in place with vulnerable countries, including Sri Lanka, Bangladesh, Egypt, and Jordan, and expects to discuss augmenting these arrangements next week with nations facing heightened pressures.

However, Georgieva warned that the world’s ability to respond is constrained by years of mounting debt. Global borrowing and debt service costs have climbed significantly, leaving nations with limited fiscal flexibility.

“Now we are faced with another shock with very little space to act upon it,” she said, underscoring the challenge policymakers face in cushioning the economic blow.

data.title: As investors rotate out of mega caps, these 10 small-cap Strong Buys are worth watching
data.description:
The AI trade could be about to broaden beyond mega-cap tech (MAGS), according to a new note from Wells Fargo Equity Research that points to small caps (IJR) as potential beneficiaries of the next phase of artificial intelligence (AIEQ) adoption.

In light of this, below is a list of the top 10 small-cap stocks with A+ EPS revision grades, ranked according to their Seeking Alpha Quant Ratings. Each of these stocks has earned a Strong Buy rating from the Quant system, signaling favorable quantitative profiles across key performance metrics.

Alto Ingredients (ALTO) and Ironwood Pharmaceuticals (IRWD) lead the pack with near-perfect Quant Ratings of 4.99 and 4.97, respectively. Oil States International (OIS) and GigaCloud Technology (GCT) follow closely behind, rounding out the top four positions.

Other notable entries include Heritage Insurance Holdings (HRTG), Postal Realty Trust (PSTL), and Herbalife (HLF). All top ten stocks in this group maintain Strong Buy quantitative profiles with scores above 4.75, reflecting positive momentum across valuation, growth, and profitability measures.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating system grades stocks on their relative performance across critical quantitative measures, including valuation, growth, stock momentum, and profitability. Ratings are given on a scale from 1 to 5, with any rating of 3.5 or above considered bullish and any rating of 2.5 or below considered bearish.

Here is the list:

  1. Alto Ingredients, Inc. (ALTO), Quant Rating: 4.99

  2. Ironwood Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (IRWD), Quant Rating: 4.97

  3. Oil States International, Inc. (OIS), Quant Rating: 4.92

  4. GigaCloud Technology Inc. (GCT), Quant Rating: 4.85

  5. Heritage Insurance Holdings, Inc. (HRTG), Quant Rating: 4.84

  6. Postal Realty Trust, Inc. (PSTL), Quant Rating: 4.83

  7. Herbalife Ltd. (HLF), Quant Rating: 4.81

  8. Alpine Income Property Trust, Inc. (PINE), Quant Rating: 4.80

  9. Movado Group, Inc. (MOV), Quant Rating: 4.79

  10. Mitek Systems, Inc. (MITK), Quant Rating: 4.79

Small-Cap ETFs: (IJR), (IWM), (VB), (VBR), (VBK), (SCHA), (SPSM), and (DFAS)

data.title: Fed ends 2018 forex trading enforcement action against Goldman Sachs
data.description:
The Federal Reserve Board said it has terminated the 2018 enforcement action against Goldman Sachs (GS) over the company’s foreign exchange trading business.

On May 1, 2018, the Fed said it fined the banking giant $54.75M for unsafe and unsound practices in its forex trading business.

The board found deficiencies in the company's internal controls and oversight of traders who buy and sell U.S. dollars and foreign currencies for the firm's own accounts and customers.

The Fed’s order required GS to improve its controls and compliance risk management. 

The regulator terminated the enforcement action coordinated with the New York State Department of Financial Services.
